content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                                    Date : March 27, 1980


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION              A-80-03-049
         Dwight Dickerson

Subject: Legally required participation in order to exercise
         a veto


         I brought the following question to the Opinion Request
meeting of March 6, 1980.  Does the doctrine of legally
required participation apply to the veto of a proposed
ordinance by the Mayor of San Francisco if the ordinance
could financially affect the Mayor within the meaning of
Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103.

         It was concluded by the staff, that if the "decision"
to veto could not be lawfully delegated, the Mayor could
exercise the veto under the legally required participation
doctrine. I was instructed by the participants of the meeting
to review the City Charter.

         According to Section 25 of the City Charter, the Mayor's
duties can be delegated only under the following circum-
stances: If the Mayor is absent from the City, the Mayor
may designated one of the supervisors to act in his or her
absence.  If the Mayor fails to make such a delegation,
the Board of Supervisors may elect one of its members as
acting Mayor.  In cases of disaster which causes the Mayor
to be absent or unavailable, and the Board of Supervisors for
some reason cannot elect one of their own members, the
order of succession is as follows:

         1)  President of the Board of Supervisors
         2)  Chairman of the Finance Committee of the Board of
             Supervisors
         3)  Senior member of the Board of Supervisors
         4)  Chief Administrative Officer of the County

File
March 27, 1980
Page Two


     The Charter does not give the Mayor authority to
delegate his responsibilities in the event of a
conflict of interest in the governmental decision.
Therefore, the legally required participation doctrine
is applicable to a decision to veto the ordinance because
the decision cannot be lawfully delegated.

DED:nrw

State of California

Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Meeting Participants                Date : March 4, 1980


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Dwight Dickerson

Subject: Legally Required Participation

       It is expected that San Francisco will be enacting
a rent control measure. The Mayor owns an apartment
building that will be affected by the measure. Government
Code Sections 87100 and 87103(b) would require her to
refrain from participating in the matter. However,
Government Code Section 87101 would allow the Mayor to
participate in the decision regarding rent control if
her participation was legally required for the decision
to be made.

       The question presented is whether she can rely on
the legally required participation doctrine for the
purpose of vetoing the measure. Only the Mayor can
veto the measure. She cannot delegate her authority
unless she is out of the city. The charter does not
provide for delegation of her authority in the case of
conflict of interests.

DD:plh

[illegible] to the

1) No N.I.R [illegible]

2) [illegible] S.F. [illegible]

sections.question: Does the doctrine of legally required participation apply to the veto of a proposed ordinance by the Mayor of San Francisco if the ordinance could financially affect the Mayor within the meaning of Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103.
sections.conclusion: If the 'decision' to veto could not be lawfully delegated, the Mayor could exercise the veto under the legally required participation doctrine. The legally required participation doctrine is applicable to a decision to veto the ordinance because the decision cannot be lawfully delegated. The Charter does not give the Mayor authority to delegate her responsibilities in the event of a conflict of interest in the governmental decision.
sections.question_synthetic: Can the San Francisco Mayor rely on the legally required participation doctrine under Government Code Section 87101 to participate in and veto a rent control ordinance that would financially affect her personal property despite conflict of interest restrictions in Sections 87100 and 87103?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Mayor may rely on the legally required participation doctrine to veto the ordinance because the veto power cannot be lawfully delegated under the San Francisco City Charter, which only permits delegation during the Mayor's absence from the city or in disaster situations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two memoranda discussing the same legal question. The second (March 4, 1980) presents the question; the first (March 27, 1980) presents the staff conclusion after Charter review. Both are integrated into the extraction. No formal CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section heading present, but clear conclusion stated in text.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the doctrine of legally required participation apply to the veto of a proposed ordinance by the Mayor of San Francisco if the ordinance could financially affect the Mayor within the meaning of Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103.

CONCLUSION: If the 'decision' to veto could not be lawfully delegated, the Mayor could exercise the veto under the legally required participation doctrine. The legally required participation doctrine is applicable to a decision to veto the ordinance because the decision cannot be lawfully delegated. The Charter does not give the Mayor authority to delegate her responsibilities in the event of a conflict of interest in the governmental decision.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that the San Francisco Mayor may participate in vetoing a rent control ordinance affecting her personal property under the legally required participation doctrine, since her veto authority cannot be lawfully delegated under the City Charter's delegation provisions.
